Task
NORTH AMERICA (NA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (exceptions, new SKUs, data sync
failures)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order with order number generated in SAP
Process Note:
• "EDI & Esker are dominant for order intake (Canada & US domestic orders)"
• "OpenText is heavily used for automated entry (Export orders -- JKSEAT region)"
• "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync
failures"
• "For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/ship to and
different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with a full
container load"
• "CSV file transfers are unique to NA, used by Canada domestic for some samples and by
JKSEAT region for customer orders"
Key Comment: "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data
sync failures. For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/
ship to and different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with
a full container load."
Unique Aspect: CSV file processing unique to North America; OpenText boundary system
for export orders; Frequent manual review due to diversity of intake channels and customer
changes
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 usage already established; Need to ensure Esker and
OpenText integration validated in S/4HANA; CSV file handling process must be maintained
 
MEXICO
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service, QSR, Retail), SAP VA01 (Assumption), Edicom/EDI
(Retail), Gmail (Food service)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service via Thalamus) / Automated (EDI for retail)
McCain    5|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System order generated by platform (Thalamus/EDI/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is the main platform for food service and QSR; Edicom EDI is used for retail"
• "Manual entry is common, especially for food service; EDI is automated for retail"
• "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer communication"
• "Order acknowledgment can be automated or manual, and may be requested by the
customer"
• "Common check points: price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate order, requested delivery
date, etc."
Key Comment: "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer
communication"
Unique Aspect: Gmail extensively used for food service operational communications;
Thalamus serves multiple channels (Food service, QSR, Retail); Edicom EDI specific to retail
channel
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus and Edicom EDI integrations must be validated;
Gmail-based workflow formalization needed; VA01 standard transaction confirmed
 
CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean)
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI (Retail), Day & Ross (DRTN
for NA-sourced)
Manual/Automated: Manual (frequent) / Automated (EDI for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record in SAP
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; SAP for system entry"
• "Manual entry is frequent, especially for exports and smaller segments"
• "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks"
• "NA-sourced orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with full traceability for export
compliance"
Key Comment: "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks" and "NA-sourced
orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
Unique Aspect: Export-focused operations with enhanced documentation requirements; Day
& Ross (DRTN) integration for NA-sourced orders; Thalamus for food service segment
SAP Migration Consideration: Export documentation workflows must be preserved; DRTN
integration for NA-sourced orders needs validation; Manual export handling processes require
clear procedures in S/4HANA
 
ANDEAN (Colombia)
System/Tool: Thalamus, Carvajal, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI
Manual/Automated: Manual (portal handling) / Automated (EDI/Carvajal for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "Thalamus and Carvajal are primary intake platforms; SAP is used for system entry"
McCain    6|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Manual portal handling, especially for food service (Actualmente se esta migrando a
Thalamus)"
• "EDI/Carvajal standards apply for retail and exports"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with data retention for
compliance"
Key Comment: "Manual portal handling, especially for food service.(Actualmente se esta
migrando a Thalamus)"
Unique Aspect: Carvajal platform specific to Andean region for retail; Migration to Thalamus
in progress; EDI/Carvajal standards for retail and exports
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus migration ongoing - ensure smooth transition;
Carvajal integration must be validated in S/4HANA; Data retention for compliance mandatory
 
BRAZIL
System/Tool: SAP Fiori (Primary), SAP ECC VA01 (Exceptions: CPU, McD, BK, maritime,
donations/samples), EDI, Neo Grid (Retail)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fiori entries) / Automated (EDI, Neo Grid)
Performer: CSR (Fiori) / SALES ADM Team (SAP ECC exceptions)
Output: SAP order generated in Fiori or ECC depending on scenario
Process Note:
• "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used for special
cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)"
• "SALES ADM team manages SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
• "WhatsApp/email are common for customer communication and error resolution"
• "Proforma invoices for CPU orders are issued via SAP ECC VF01/VF02"
• "Strong SAP Fiori adoption; exceptions handled manually in ECC"
Key Comment: "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used
for special cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)" and "SALES ADM team manages
SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
Unique Aspect: Dual system architecture (Fiori primary + ECC exceptions); SALES ADM team
handles special scenarios; Strong Fiori adoption among all LATAM regions; WhatsApp business
communication; Proforma invoice generation (VF01/VF02)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual system (Fiori + ECC) needs consolidation
strategy in S/4HANA; Special handling for CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples must be
accommodated; SALES ADM team workflows require clear definition; Proforma invoice process
(VF01/VF02) validation
 
URUGUAY
System/Tool: Thalamus, Power Street, EDI, SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (domestic via Thalamus/Power Street) / Automated (EDI retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "EDI, Thalamus, and Power Street are used for order intake; Power Street is unique to
Uruguay"
• "Manual entry is common for domestic orders; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and email are standard for customer communication and error resolution"
McCain    7|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Power Street: Tool to input customer orders for direct and indirect sales. This is used to
load orders on every truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest
of McCain. Orders are loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Key Comment: "Power Street is unique to Uruguay" and "This is used to load orders on every
truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest of McCain. Orders are
loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Unique Aspect: Power Street ONLY exists in Uruguay; On-the-spot truck loading model;
Unique sales operational model different from rest of McCain; Direct and indirect sales via
mobile tool
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Power Street unique to Uruguay must be integrated
or replaced in S/4HANA; Mobile truck loading workflow is business-critical; Operational model
significantly different from other regions
 
ARGENTINA
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), Krikos360/EDI (Retail), Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone (QSR),
SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service, QSR) / Automated (EDI retail via Krikos360)
Performer: CSR / Operations (varies by channel)
Output: System order by platform (Thalamus/Krikos360/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; Krikos360/EDI for retail; Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone for
QSR"
• "Manual entry is standard for food service and QSR; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication"
• "Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy; EDI for retail"
Key Comment: "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication" and
"Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy"
Unique Aspect: Krikos360 platform (Planexware) specific to Argentina retail; Multi-channel
digital communication (Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone) for QSR; Channel-specific tool strategy
SAP Migration Consideration: Krikos360/Planexware integration validation critical; WhatsApp/
Gmail workflow formalization needed; Multi-channel tool landscape requires consolidation
assessment
 
APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand)
System/Tool: Portalink, EDI, Email, SAP VA02/VA03 (NOT VA01 for EDI)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Portalink) / Manual (email-based changes)
Performer: Customer Solutions Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders, regardless of
how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on email"
• "Portalink Integration: Serves as critical boundary system converting non-EDI orders into
SAP-compatible format, enabling immediate automated acknowledgements for non-EDI
customers"
• "ANZ: VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
• "HighRadius Financial Integration: Used for credit checks and payment tracking"
• "Excel Macro Automation: End-of-day order confirmations automated through Excel
macros"
McCain    8|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Z7 CSR Hold Implementation: Universal application of Z7 CSR hold enables manual review
and allocation rule enforcement by commercial team"
Key Comment: "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders,
regardless of how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on
email" and "VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
Unique Aspect: CRITICAL: VA01 NOT used for EDI order entry - ANZ uses VA02/VA03;
Portalink boundary system converts non-EDI to SAP format; HighRadius for credit/payment;
Excel macro for end-of-day confirmations; Z7 CSR Hold universal; Duplicate EDI order manual
deletion required
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - ANZ does NOT use VA01 for EDI orders (uses VA02/
VA03) - This is UNIQUE and must be carefully evaluated in S/4HANA migration; Portalink
boundary system integration critical; HighRadius integration validation; Excel macro process
may need modernization; Z7 CSR Hold implementation; ATP functionality gap (currently no ATP)
 
APAC-CHINA
System/Tool: Digital Hub (Cloud-based portal - MANDATORY for ALL orders), SAP VA01 (via
EDI integration from Digital Hub)
Manual/Automated: Manual entry into Digital Hub / Automated (Digital Hub to SAP via EDI)
Performer: Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order triggered via EDI from Digital Hub after validation
Process Note:
• "All customer purchase orders (retail or food service channels) are received and processed
through the cloud-based Digital Hub portal"
• "Orders are manually entered into the Digital Hub, where validations and changes are
performed before triggering sales orders into SAP via EDI integration"
• "No Direct Customer EDI: All orders flow through Digital Hub regardless of customer
technical capability"
• "Real-time Integration: Direct integration with warehouse management systems provides
accurate, current inventory data for ATP decisions"
• "Auto-cancellation Logic: Sophisticated cancellation workflows with reason codes (Y1, Y5) for
unpaid orders enable automated revenue protection"
• "ZF delivery blocks preferred over credit blocks for payment terms enforcement"
Key Comment: "All customer purchase orders (retail or food service channels) are received and
processed through the cloud-based Digital Hub portal" and "Orders are manually entered into
the Digital Hub, where validations and changes are performed before triggering sales orders
into SAP via EDI integration"
Unique Aspect: Digital Hub MANDATORY for 100% of orders regardless of channel; No
direct customer-to-SAP EDI; Dual ATP (Digital Hub "light" + SAP "final"); ZF delivery block for
prepayment instead of credit blocks; Auto-cancel Y5 rejection code for unpaid prepay orders;
WMS real-time integration; Channel-specific CRM (Food Service) vs email (Retail/QSR)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Digital Hub is mandatory gateway (no direct SAP
access); Digital Hub to S/4HANA EDI integration must be validated; Dual ATP mechanism must
be maintained; ZF delivery block logic; Auto-cancellation workflows (Y1, Y5); WMS integration
validation; Prepayment enforcement logic
 
APAC-INDIA
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI via OpenText (Reliance ONLY), Email, Spreadsheet,
Bheem DMS
McCain    9|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI for select modern trade) / Manual (DC staff entry for non-
EDI)
Performer: DC Executives / Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders from select customers in modern trade (e.g.,: Reliance) are automatically
integrated into SAP via OpenText, while non-EDI orders received by email or spreadsheet are
manually entered by distribution center staff"
• "DC-based Processing: Decentralized order entry model contrasts with other regions'
centralized approaches"
• "Minimal Automation: Limited automated order acknowledgements (only Reliance) reflects
low digitalization maturity"
• "Reliance Special Treatment: Only customer receiving automated order acknowledgements,
indicating strategic partnership requirements"
• "Manual-first Philosophy: Preference for manual processes over automation, possibly due
to complexity and control requirements"
• "MB5T used for stock in transit checks, crucial for multi-location inventory management"
Key Comment: "EDI orders from select customers in modern trade (e.g.,: Reliance) are
automatically integrated into SAP via OpenText, while non-EDI orders received by email or
spreadsheet are manually entered by distribution center staff" and "Only customer receiving
automated order acknowledgements (Reliance)"
Unique Aspect: Decentralized DC-based order entry model; ONLY Reliance has EDI and
automated acknowledgement; Manual-first philosophy; OpenText for select modern
trade EDI; Export orders processed in monthly cycles; MB5T for stock in transit checks
(multi-location); Bheem DMS for retail claims; 100% credit block by default; FSSAI license
validation at order creation; TDP days (10/20/30) affect transport capacity
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Decentralized DC-based entry model unique to India;
OpenText integration for Reliance EDI; Manual entry by DC staff workflows; Minimal automation
- high manual dependency; MB5T stock in transit functionality; FSSAI license validation must be
maintained; Default credit block process; TDP days operational logic
 
APAC-JKSEAT (Japan, Korea, SE Asia & Taiwan)
System/Tool:
• Japan: SAP VA01/ME21 (Assumption), Fax, Customer websites, EDI, CSV macro
• KSEAT: SAP VA01 (Assumption), CSV files, Macro/EDI watcher, Email
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fax, websites in Japan) / Automated (CSV macro, EDI for both)
Performer: Customer Service Team
Output: SAP sales order, Proforma invoice (as order confirmation)
Process Note:
• Japan: "Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer websites,
EDI, and CSV files. Fax and website orders are manually entered into SAP , while EDI and CSV
file orders are automatically integrated into SAP"
• KSEAT: "Orders are received via email as either CSV files or manual requests. CSV files are
uploaded to a shared folder, then picked up by EDI or a macro for automatic entry into SAP ,
while manual orders are entered directly into SAP"
• "Traditional Communication Channels: Japan maintains fax communication alongside
modern channels, reflecting local business culture and customer preferences"
McCain    10|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Advanced Order Planning: Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance eliminates need
for real-time inventory visibility but requires sophisticated planning"
• "CSV Automation: KSEAT uses shared folder mechanism with macro/EDI pickup for CSV
automation"
• "Proforma Invoice as confirmation: KC sends Proforma Invoice (manual PDF from SAP) as
order confirmation; HAVI/McDonald's excluded"
• "No local ATP/MOQ gating at JKC; orders are taken weeks to months ahead (min ~6 weeks),
and sourcing regions (e.g., NA, EU) run ATP on their side"
Key Comment: "Japan: Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer
websites, EDI, and CSV files" and "Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance" and "No local
ATP/MOQ gating at JKC"
Unique Aspect: Traditional fax still used in Japan; CSV macro/shared folder automation
in KSEAT; Proforma invoice used as order confirmation (not standard acknowledgement);
No local ATP - sourcing regions handle ATP; Long lead times (6 weeks to 4 months); ME21 for
purchase orders alongside VA01; Power BI for shipment tracking; Regional orders 6-16 weeks
ahead of ship date
SAP Migration Consideration: Japan VA01/ME21 usage must be validated; Fax integration/
workflow documentation needed; CSV macro automation from shared folders; Proforma invoice
generation process; No local ATP - sourcing region dependency; Long planning horizon (6
weeks-4 months) must be supported; Power BI integration for tracking
 
GREAT BRITAIN (GB)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption for order creation), EDI, Esker, Email (Manual), ZV05N
(for display)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (email orders)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: Manually entered order (from email) / Automated order from EDI/Esker
Process Note:
• "GB receives orders through three primary channels: EDI (automated), email (manual entry
required), and Esker (PDF document processing)"
• "Transaction codes used: VA01 for order creation, ZV05N for displaying orders, VA02 for
order amendments"
• "EDI orders undergo only basic sense checks; no comprehensive price validation at entry"
• "if there is an error in the EDI it will generate a failed idoc which can be viewed in WE05"
• "Incompletion log in SAP flags missing data, causing EDI orders to fail and go on hold,
equally if the PO is a duplicate SAP puts the order on hold"
• "Manual orders similarly have no price check at entry; incompletion log identifies gaps"
• "Manual process only - no automatic customer notification upon order entry"
• "All orders entered using t-code VA01, where the order type is specified"
Key Comment: "GB receives orders through three primary channels: EDI (automated), email
(manual entry required), and Esker (PDF document processing)" and "Manual process only - no
automatic customer notification upon order entry" and "All orders entered using t-code VA01,
where the order type is specified"
Unique Aspect: ZV05N used for displaying orders (alongside VA03); WE05 for failed IDoc
monitoring; Esker reads PDF documents from customers; No automatic acknowledgement
- manual email confirmation only; No price validation at entry - handled later as claims via
AR inbox; Incompletion log flags missing data; Duplicate PO auto-hold; EDI MDN available on
customer request
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 standard usage confirmed; ZV05N display transaction
validation in S/4HANA; WE05 IDoc monitoring; Esker PDF integration; Manual acknowledgement
McCain    11|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
process; No price validation at entry - pricing claims handled downstream; Incompletion log
dependency
 
EMENA (Europe, Middle East, North Africa)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), ZVA05N (import transaction), EDI, Esker, CSV process
(Italy), Manual (email/phone)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker, CSV Italy) / Manual (email/phone)
Performer: Customer Service Representative / System (for off-time import)
Output: Orders imported into SAP
Process Note:
• "Order requests could be received via various platforms: Manual entry (email & phone call),
ESKER (reads PDF documents from customers), EDI, CSV process (Italy) via EDI"
• "Orders from various channels are imported into SAP system during off time"
• "Only applicable to EMENA"
• T-codes mentioned: "VA01, VA02, ZVA05N, ZG_MTD_SHIPMENT, VL01N, VL02N, VL06"
Key Comment: "Orders from various channels are imported into SAP system during off time"
and "Only applicable to EMENA"
Unique Aspect: ZVA05N import transaction ONLY in EMENA; Off-time import process
unique to this region; CSV process specific to Italy via EDI; Esker PDF document processing;
Multi-channel intake (EDI, Esker, CSV, Manual); Import batch processing during off-peak hours
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - ZVA05N off-time import transaction unique to
EMENA must be evaluated for S/4HANA equivalent; Italy CSV process via EDI; Esker integration;
Batch import scheduling and timing; Multi-channel consolidation during off-time import
 
SOUTH AFRICA (SA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Email (Direct), EDI via Vector (Indirect 3PL)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Direct Network) / Automated (Indirect Network via Vector EDI)
Performer: Customer Service Representative (Direct) / Vector System (Indirect)
Output: Manually entered order (Direct) / EDI order via Vector (Indirect)
Process Note:
• "Purchase requests received via email" (Direct Network)
• "Direct Network: Orders received manually by delivery network"
• "Indirect Network (3PLs/Vector): EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from
customers; next day, orders communicated to McCain for backward integration into SAP
(boundary system), operating on a consignment model"
• "(Direct) Customer Service receives purchase requests via email or manual channels and
manually enters into SAP . T-code VA01 used for order creation"
• "(Indirect) EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from customers"
• "Proforma invoice/manual order confirmation for some customers"
Key Comment: "Direct Network: Orders received manually by delivery network" and "Indirect
Network (3PLs/Vector): EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from customers; next
day, orders communicated to McCain for backward integration into SAP (boundary system),
operating on a consignment model"
Unique Aspect: Dual network model - Direct vs Indirect (3PL/Vector); Vector 3PL ONLY in
South Africa; Consignment model for indirect network; Next-day backward integration to
SAP; Proforma invoice for some customers; T-code VA01 confirmed for order creation; Manual
entry for direct network
McCain    12|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual network model (Direct manual vs Indirect
Vector EDI); Vector 3PL boundary system integration critical; Consignment model logic; Next-day
backward integration timing and process; Proforma invoice generation for specific customers;
VA01 standard usage
(as-is) IT System: SAP , Digital Hub, Excel(CSV), Thalamus
IT Systems:
- SAP ECC
SAP/SSC/Executable: SAP - VA01, VA02
Organization units:  01.05.01.03 Enter Order Into System - Customer Solutions/3PL
Dictionary entries: SAP ECC
Task type: Receive
Enter Consignment Details into the ERP
Task
NORTH AMERICA (NA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (exceptions, new SKUs, data sync
failures)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order with order number generated in SAP

the same business in real time.

SAP ECC (Enterprise Central Component)
SAP ECC (ERP Central Component) is a comprehensive enterprise resource planning (ERP)
software suite developed by SAP . It serves as the core component of many organizations’ IT
landscapes, integrating key business processes and data across departments, and enabling
efficient management of resources, operations, and reporting.Key 
Features of SAP ECC:Centralized database for seamless data flow across business functions
• Modular structure, allowing organizations to implement only the modules they need
• Real-time processing and reporting capabilities
• Strong integration with other SAP and third-party systems
SAP CO, a module within SAP ECC that focuses on internal accounting and management
accounting, providing tools for planning, controlling, and analyzing costs and revenues. It is tightly
integrated with SAP FI (Financial Accounting) and is often referred to as SAP FICO. SAP CO helps
businesses track costs associated with various business processes, allocate costs, and manage
profitability analysis.

refined_blueprint.strategy_report_markdown:
# Re-Imagined Strategy Report: Order Intake

1. Executive Summary — One Big Move synthesis
The One Big Move is to introduce a global Side-Car System of Intelligence that normalizes multi-channel order intake, applies deterministic validations and routing, and orchestrates ERP posting while keeping the ERP kernel clean and configuration-led. This hub-and-spoke operating model addresses pervasive manual rekeying, exception handling, and acknowledgement gaps evidenced across regions and channels (email, WhatsApp, fax, portals, EDI) [F-001, F-003, F-004, F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-026, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2]. Deterministic rules will standardize export attributes, order-type branching, Z7 CSR holds, and EDI failure triage within core configuration, while agent personas in the Side-Car handle unstructured intake (email/WhatsApp/fax), change requests, and dispute/deduction evidence reconciliation [F-002, F-019, F-034, F-036; F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-040]. The Trust Gap Protocol starts in Shadow mode to safeguard operations, using confidence levels from Phase 2 path decisions to move toward Co-Pilot and Autopilot only when evidence-backed reliability is achieved [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow; PATH DECISIONS confidences].

2. Cognitive Friction Analysis — The friction table (reproduce as-is)

4. Architecture of the Future State — Hub-and-spoke operating model with agent personas
Architecture principle: Clean Core is enforced end-to-end; all custom logic, perception models, and orchestration reside in a Side-Car System of Intelligence. The ERP kernel (SAP ECC/VA01/VA02/VA03) remains configuration-led for validations, order-type branching, Z7 CSR holds, output determination, and IDoc-based posting [F-019, F-035, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].

Hub-and-spoke model:
- Hub: Side-Car Order Intake Orchestrator
  - Functions: multi-channel intake normalization (email, WhatsApp, fax, CSV, portal payloads), deterministic validations, path routing (A/B/C), exception queuing, acknowledgement dispatch, and ERP posting via integration contracts [F-004, F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-024, F-026, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Clean Core enforcement: no custom code in ERP kernel; all AI, parsing, and workflow logic live in the Side-Car [Clean Core principle; supports ERP rules for order type and Z7 holds per DOC1 references F-019, F-036].
- Spokes (intake channels and boundary systems):
  - EDI/IDoc spoke: deterministic intake for standard orders; IDoc failure triage via Side-Car using error-code rules [F-001, F-034; DOC1].
  - Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360 spoke: structured channel integration; Side-Car applies validations and routes exceptions [F-001, F-003, F-016, F-026, F-028; DOC1].
  - Digital Hub (China) spoke: mandatory gateway; Side-Car integrates to reduce manual portal entry and automate validations ahead of SAP posting [F-020, F-037; DOC1].
  - Thalamus/Carvajal/Neo Grid spoke: food service portals; Side-Car maps portal orders deterministically into ERP [F-005, F-009, F-011; DOC1].
  - Power Street mobile spoke: truck-loading mobile orders; Side-Car normalizes payload variability before posting [F-014, F-038; DOC1].
  - Vector (SA indirect) spoke: reconciliation with next-day backward integration; Side-Car coordinates and surfaces exceptions [F-039; DOC1].
  - Unstructured channels: email, WhatsApp, fax, phone, customer websites; Side-Car uses agent personas to parse and normalize [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-026, F-028, F-029, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Agent personas in the Side-Car:
  - Intake Scribe: OCR/NLP-enabled extraction for email/WhatsApp/fax/phone transcripts; transforms unstructured artifacts into normalized order payloads with confidence tagging [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Intent Analyzer: deterministic classification and routing for order type (standard vs consignment), Z7 CSR holds, export incoterms fields, and duplicate detection [F-002, F-018, F-019, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Dispute Judge: triage for deductions/claims requiring evidence reconciliation across systems; assembles case context and recommendations for CSR approval [F-040; DOC1].

ERP responsibilities (configuration-led):
- Order-type branching and validations remain in ERP config, not custom code [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Z7 CSR hold enforcement and allocation rules via standard gating, with Side-Car triggering and monitoring [F-019; DOC1].
- Output determination for proforma invoicing and acknowledgements configured in ERP or standard middleware outputs; Side-Car orchestrates dispatch [F-013, F-025, F-027; DOC1].

Traceability to evidence:
- Hub-and-spoke integrates every listed regional channel, aligning with documented systems and manual steps [F-001–F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
- Agent personas are scoped to replace manual steps where unstructured content and reasoning are documented frictions [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-040; DOC1; DOC2].

5. Technical Stack — System of Intelligence, System of Record, integration contracts
System of Record:
- ERP: SAP ECC with transactions VA01/VA02/VA03 and billing VF01/VF02, serving as the authoritative order record and posting target [F-011, F-013, F-017, F-026, F-035; DOC1].
- EDI/IDoc processing: ERP-side IDoc channels for automated ingestion and error handling with deterministic rules [F-034; DOC1].

System of Intelligence (Side-Car):
- Intake adapters: connectors for EDI/Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360, Digital Hub (China), Thalamus, Carvajal, Neo Grid, Power Street, Vector, and unstructured sources (email, WhatsApp, fax, phone, customer websites) [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-009–F-012, F-014–F-016, F-020–F-026, F-028–F-029, F-037–F-039; DOC1; DOC2].
- Parsing and normalization: AI-enabled services embedded in Side-Car for unstructured channels; deterministic mappers for structured channel payloads [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Validation engine: business-rule services for price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate checks, requested delivery date, and export attributes; implemented outside ERP to preserve Clean Core [F-002, F-006, F-018, F-019, F-036; DOC1].
- Acknowledgement and notification orchestration: standard dispatchers handling automated acknowledgements, proforma confirmations, and customer notifications [F-004, F-022, F-025, F-027; DOC1].
- Case and dispute triage: Dispute Judge service for cross-system evidence reconciliation, creating recommended actions for CSR approval [F-040; DOC1].

Integration contracts:
- Staged processing contract between Side-Car and ERP:
  - Intake stage: unify payload schemas across channels (EDI, portal, mobile, unstructured) [F-001, F-003, F-014, F-020, F-033–F-038; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Classification stage: determine order type, holds, export attributes, and duplicates using deterministic rules [F-002, F-018–F-019, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Posting stage: create or amend orders via ERP API/IDoc/transactions, maintaining idempotency and Clean Core [F-017, F-035; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Acknowledgement stage: issue confirmations via configured outputs, avoiding manual email steps [F-004, F-022, F-025, F-027; DOC1].
- China-specific gateway contract: enforce Digital Hub as a mandatory upstream stage and align validations to reduce manual entry in Digital Hub before SAP posting [F-020, F-037; DOC1].
- Uruguay mobile contract: normalize Power Street payloads and support same-day posting workflows [F-014, F-038; DOC1].
- South Africa reconciliation contract: schedule Vector backward integration reconciliation and surface exceptions next day [F-039; DOC1].

Clean Core enforcement:
- All custom logic (AI parsing, cross-channel normalization, workflow orchestration) is strictly in the Side-Car; ERP uses only configuration and standard interfaces [Clean Core principle; aligns with evidence of ERP transactions and IDoc workflows in DOC1/DOC2].

6. Integration Design Deep Dive — Staged processing contract (intake, classification, ERP posting)
Stage 0: Intake and normalization (Side-Car)
- Channel adapters receive orders and change requests from EDI/Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360, Digital Hub (China), Thalamus/Carvajal/Neo Grid, Power Street, Vector, and unstructured sources (email, WhatsApp, fax, phone, websites) [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-009–F-012, F-014–F-016, F-020–F-026, F-028–F-029, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Unstructured parsing via Intake Scribe converts email/WhatsApp/fax/phone/web artifacts into normalized payloads with confidence scores and extracted fields (sold-to, ship-to, SKU, quantity, incoterms, RDD) [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Digital Hub (China) intake respects mandatory gateway, reducing manual typing by integrating upload/mapping from Side-Car where allowable by the portal’s interface [F-020, F-037; DOC1].

Stage 1: Deterministic classification (Side-Car)
- Order-type determination (standard vs consignment) based on business rules, removing inconsistent manual branching [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Z7 CSR hold rules applied deterministically before ERP posting, standardizing allocation enforcement [F-019; DOC1].
- Export-specific attributes (incoterms when same sold-to/ship-to; samples with full container load) standardized via core rules [F-002; DOC1].
- Duplicate detection for EDI orders preventing manual deletion and accidental removal [F-018; DOC1].

Stage 2: Validation (Side-Car and ERP configuration)
- Price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate checks, requested delivery date validations applied deterministically; ERP retains core validations; Side-Car performs pre-validation to reduce SAP error cycles [F-006; DOC1].
- EDI/data-quality exception triage: use error-code-based rules to route and resolve failures; improve first pass automation [F-034; DOC1].

Stage 3: ERP posting (Clean Core)
- Sales order creation/amendment via standard interfaces (IDoc/API) or transactions VA01/VA02/VA03 as per region-specific process, avoiding any custom ERP kernel code [F-017, F-026, F-035; DOC1; DOC2].
- China: Side-Car triggers EDI-based creation after Digital Hub validations, preserving mandatory gateway compliance [F-020, F-037; DOC1].
- Uruguay: Side-Car posts normalized Power Street orders same-day with idempotent keys to prevent duplicates [F-014, F-038; DOC1].

Stage 4: Acknowledgements and outputs
- Automated order acknowledgements replace Gmail/manual steps across regions [F-004, F-022, F-027; DOC1].
- Proforma invoice generation for CPU and KC confirmations via configured outputs and automated dispatch, removing manual PDF handling [F-013, F-025; DOC1].

Stage 5: Exceptions, changes, and disputes
- Changes based on email instructions parsed by Intake Scribe and routed to deterministic VA02/VA03 updates; CSR approves if confidence is below threshold [F-017, F-035; DOC1; DOC2].
- Dispute/Deduction triage assembles cross-system evidence and proposes actions for CSR adjudication [F-040; DOC1].

Idempotency and deduplication baked into every stage to address duplicate EDI orders and repeated manual touchpoints documented in the process graphs [F-018, F-030–F-031; DOC1; DOC2].

7. Agent Persona Reasoning Model — Intake Scribe, Intent Analyzer, Dispute Judge boundaries
Intake Scribe (Perception + Extraction):
- Scope: unstructured order intake and change requests from email, WhatsApp, fax, phone transcripts, and customer websites [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Output: normalized order/change payloads with field-level confidence and provenance.
- Boundary: does not post to ERP; hands off to Intent Analyzer and ERP workflow per Trust Gap phase (Shadow) [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow].

Intent Analyzer (Deterministic Classification + Routing):
- Scope: order-type classification (standard vs consignment), Z7 CSR hold enforcement, export attributes normalization, duplicate detection [F-002, F-018–F-019, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Output: classification decisions and routing to ERP posting or exception queues.
- Boundary: relies on rules; no custom logic in ERP kernel; executes fully in Side-Car.

Dispute Judge (Evidence Triage + Recommendation):
- Scope: deduction and dispute triage requiring cross-system reconciliation and compliance context assembly [F-040; DOC1].
- Output: recommended resolution actions, evidence bundles, and CSR decision support.
- Boundary: never auto-posts financial adjustments; operates under Shadow/Co-Pilot until confidence thresholds are met.

Agent-to-path alignment:
- Path A: ERP configuration-led determinations (e.g., order-type, Z7 hold); Intent Analyzer orchestrates using deterministic rules [F-019, F-036; PATH A rationales].
- Path B: deterministic integrations/validations; Intake Scribe may help normalize structured payloads; Intent Analyzer enforces rules [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-007–F-011, F-014, F-016–F-018, F-020–F-029, F-030–F-035, F-037, F-039; PATH B rationales].
- Path C: perception and adaptive handling required; Intake Scribe leads with NLP/OCR; Dispute Judge engages for cross-system evidence [F-012, F-015, F-033, F-038, F-040; PATH C rationales].

8. The Trust Gap Protocol — Shadow / Co-Pilot / Autopilot phases with confidence thresholds
Phases:
- Shadow: agents observe, parse, and recommend; no autonomous ERP postings; CSRs retain full control [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow]. Apply recommendations for unstructured intake and changes with confidence tags derived from Path Decisions (e.g., 90%–97% confidences).
- Co-Pilot: agents propose actions and pre-stage transactions; CSR approves before posting. Trigger Co-Pilot for cases where confidence regularly meets or exceeds documented levels (e.g., 90%+ for deterministic validations; 97%+ for structured EDI triage per Path Decisions) [PATH DECISIONS confidences].
- Autopilot: agents act without human approval on deterministic flows where evidence and historical performance sustain high confidence (e.g., >97% confidence for duplicates and EDI exception rule triage per Path Decisions) [F-018, F-034; PATH DECISIONS confidences].

Confidence thresholds (evidence-linked):
- Deterministic validations and routing: adopt Co-Pilot at ≥90% confidence; consider Autopilot only when ≥97% confidence sustained and exceptions trend to zero [PATH DECISIONS entries citing 90% and 97%].
- Unstructured intake parsing (email/WhatsApp/fax): remain in Shadow until ≥97% extraction confidence consistently achieved and CSR override rates materially decline [F-012, F-015, F-023, F-033; PATH C confidences].
- Dispute triage: Shadow-to-Co-Pilot only; Autopilot gated due to compliance sensitivity, regardless of confidence [F-040].

Clean Core guardrails:
- Even in Autopilot, ERP kernel remains configuration-only; Side-Car executes orchestration and posts via standard interfaces [DOC1; DOC2].

- F-003 — Path B: Frequent reviews across structured channels can be codified as deterministic validations; no unstructured inputs or adaptive planning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-004 — Path B: Acknowledgement sending is a deterministic workflow; can be standardized without perception/reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-005 — Path B: Manual rekeying from a portal can be replaced by deterministic integration/mapping; no need for perception/reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)

- F-007 — Path B: Export document generation/compliance steps can be deterministic workflows using structured order data. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-008 — Path B: Corrections/change requests can follow rule-based approval and SAP updates; no unstructured perception required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-009 — Path B: Portal-to-ERP order transfer is deterministic integration; no perception/reasoning required. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-010 — Path B: Rule-based workflows for change requests with compliance data retention can be automated deterministically. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-011 — Path A: Special orders should use standardized core ERP paths to remove dual-system handoffs; rule-based configuration applies. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-012 — Path C: Unstructured WhatsApp/email content requires perception (text parsing) and reasoning for triage; adaptive handling of varied cases. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-013 — Path A: Proforma issuance is standard ERP functionality; enforce core output determination to avoid manual steps (rule-based). Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-014 — Path B: On-the-spot truck loading orders can be integrated deterministically from the mobile channel into ERP. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-015 — Path C: Inputs via Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone are unstructured; requires perception and adaptive handling to extract and normalize orders. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-016 — Path B: Retail/hardcopy indicates unstructured documents; requires perception (OCR) and reasoning for validation. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-017 — Path B: Email-based change requests require perception to parse varied content and reasoning to apply correct SAP changes. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-018 — Path B: Duplicate detection/deletion can be enforced via deterministic rules on EDI/IDoc; no perception or complex reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-019 — Path A: Z7 CSR hold gating can be standardized with core ERP configuration/rules; deterministic enforcement. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-020 — Path B: Digital Hub validations and SAP integration are deterministic; automate portal data entry and rules. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-021 — Path B: Email orders are unstructured and require perception; spreadsheets may be structured but mixed channels imply adaptive handling. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-022 — Path B: Order acknowledgements are deterministic notifications; automate without perception/reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-023 — Path B: Fax and varied customer websites introduce unstructured inputs; requires perception and adaptive extraction. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-024 — Path B: Email order inputs are unstructured; requires perception to parse and adaptive handling to map to SAP. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-025 — Path B: Proforma confirmation dispatch is deterministic; automate generation and sending from core system. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-026 — Path B: Email orders are unstructured; requires perception to extract and adaptive action for exceptions. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-027 — Path B: Customer notification on order entry is rule-based and deterministic; no perception/reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-028 — Path C: Email/phone call intake is unstructured; requires perception and adaptive mapping to orders. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-029 — Path B: Direct Network email requests are unstructured; requires perception and adaptive handling. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-030 — Path B: Repeated manual touchpoints can be converted to deterministic workflows; no inherent need for perception or reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-031 — Path B: Exception routing can be defined with deterministic rules/workflows; adaptive AI not required if conditions are known. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-032 — Path A: Decision gateways should be standardized in core ERP process logic; rule-based branching removes variance. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-033 — Path C: Manual intake from email/PDF/fax requires perception and adaptive mapping; multi-step reasoning for exceptions. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-034 — Path B: EDI/IDoc failure triage can be deterministic (error codes, validation rules); no unstructured perception needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-035 — Path B: Order amendments via SAP transactions follow rule-based steps; workflow/RPA suitable; no perception/reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-036 — Path A: Order type classification is a core ERP decision; standardize branching rules in ERP (deterministic). Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-037 — Path B: Mandatory Digital Hub gateway implies deterministic validations/routing; automate within/around the portal. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-038 — Path C: Mobile payload variability from Power Street indicates adaptive action is needed to normalize and handle exceptions. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-039 — Path B: Next-day 3PL backward integration reconciliation is deterministic; workflow automation suffices. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-040 — Path C: Dispute/deduction triage involves cross-system evidence and likely unstructured artifacts; requires perception and reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)

10. Regional Policy Registry — JSON block of regional nuances
{
  "india_dc_based_entry": true,
  "china_digital_hub": true,
  "anz_va01_exception_enabled": true,
  "uruguay_power_street_enabled": true
}

11. Delivery and Rollout Plan — Wave 1/2/3 with KPIs, exit criteria, and governance
Delivery approach adheres to Clean Core: Side-Car is the change vehicle; ERP remains configuration-led and untouched by custom code [DOC1; DOC2].

- Wave 1: Stabilize deterministic core and standardize acknowledgements
  - Scope: Path A frictions and foundational Path B with clear deterministic rules (e.g., export fields, Z7 CSR hold, order-type branching, duplicate detection, EDI exception rule triage, automated acknowledgements, proforma outputs) [F-002, F-018–F-019, F-032, F-034; F-004, F-022, F-025, F-027; DOC1; DOC2].
  - KPIs:
    - First-pass automation rate for EDI and deterministic validations (derived from reduction in manual triage and deletion) [F-018, F-034].
    - Automated acknowledgement coverage across regions (replacement of manual Gmail/email steps) [F-004, F-022, F-027].
    - Elimination of inconsistent order-type branching by ERP configuration [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Exit criteria:
    - All Path A items operationalized with documented rules and measurable reduction of manual steps.
    - Side-Car contracts for acknowledgements and proforma outputs functioning across applicable regions [F-013, F-025].
  - Governance:
    - Business Process Owner sign-off per friction ID.
    - ERP Configuration Board approval for any rule changes, ensuring kernel cleanliness.

- Wave 2: Integrate structured portals and regional gateways
  - Scope: Path B frictions for portals and gateways (Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360, Thalamus/Carvajal/Neo Grid, Digital Hub, Vector, Power Street normalization) [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-007–F-011, F-014, F-016, F-020, F-021, F-029, F-037, F-039; DOC1].
  - KPIs:
    - Reduction in manual rekeying for food service/QSR and portal orders [F-005, F-009, F-015].
    - Digital Hub manual entry elimination rate (China) [F-020, F-037].
    - Same-day posting rate for Power Street mobile orders (idempotent, normalized) [F-014, F-038].
  - Exit criteria:
    - Side-Car adapters live for each portal/gateway with validated mappings.
    - Documented exception queues and resolutions for remaining manual steps.
  - Governance:
    - Regional SteerCos approve go-lives aligned with nuances JSON (e.g., china_digital_hub true; uruguay_power_street_enabled true).

- Wave 3: Unstructured channels and dispute triage
  - Scope: Path C frictions (WhatsApp/email intake, multi-channel PDF/fax/spreadsheet, Power Street adaptive normalization, deductions/disputes) [F-012, F-015, F-033, F-038, F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
  - KPIs:
    - Extraction confidence for email/WhatsApp/fax orders (agent-level confidence aligned with Path Decisions) [PATH C confidences].
    - CSR override rate for parsed changes and unstructured orders (trend toward Co-Pilot viability) [F-017; DOC2].
    - Dispute cycle-time reduction via evidence bundling [F-040; DOC1].
  - Exit criteria:
    - Shadow mode stable with documented confidence metrics; progression criteria to Co-Pilot defined by Trust Gap thresholds.
    - Proven triage recommendations by Dispute Judge with CSR acceptance rate targets.
  - Governance:
    - Trust Gap Council evaluates evidence to approve phase transitions; Clean Core gate enforces Side-Car-only logic.

Global governance and controls:
- A Friction ID catalog drives scope and acceptance; each ID maps to path, region, and evidence tag [F-001–F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
- Operability SRE team monitors idempotency, retries, and schema validation across stages and channels, consistent with Appendix controls.

12. Appendix: Control and Operability Baseline — Schema validation, retries, idempotency, governance
Controls (applied uniformly in Side-Car to preserve Clean Core):
- Schema validation:
  - Unified payload schema versioning for EDI, portal, mobile, and parsed unstructured content to reduce rework from format errors evidenced across diverse intake channels [F-003, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Region-specific extensions for Digital Hub, Power Street, and Vector, documented against evidence of mandatory gateways and mobile variability [F-020, F-037, F-038, F-039; DOC1].
- Idempotency:
  - Idempotent keys (e.g., source reference + customer + timestamp) to prevent duplicate ERP postings and align with duplicate EDI deletion friction [F-018; DOC1].
  - Idempotent change processing with compare-and-apply semantics for email-based changes [F-017; DOC2].
- Retries and backoff:
  - Deterministic retry policies for transient EDI/IDoc errors and portal integrations, guided by error-code triage evidence [F-034; DOC1].
  - Circuit breakers for Digital Hub and Vector integrations to avoid cascading failures [F-037, F-039; DOC1].
- Exception queues:
  - Dedicated queues for unstructured parsing low-confidence artifacts (email/WhatsApp/fax/phone), disputable changes, and compliance-sensitive cases [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
  - SLA tracking for manual acknowledgements transitioning to automated, aligned to evidence of current manual processes [F-004, F-022, F-027; DOC1].
- Governance and audit:
  - Full provenance on all transformed fields (with source artifact links) to address auditability gaps from unstructured channels [F-012, F-015; DOC1].
  - ERP remains configuration-driven; any change in validations or branching is approved by ERP Configuration Board, respecting Clean Core [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Security and compliance:
  - Data minimization and encryption for WhatsApp/email artifacts; evidence-driven need to secure unstructured communications [F-012; DOC1].
  - Export documentation workflows centralized, reducing manual preparation and compliance risk [F-007; DOC1].
- Operability dashboards:
  - Channel health, exception rates, first-pass automation, acknowledgement coverage, and dispute triage metrics mapped to friction IDs [F-001–F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
- Testing and release management:
  - Evidence-linked test cases per friction ID; dual-run Shadow mode validation before enabling Co-Pilot/Autopilot per Trust Gap [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow; PATH DECISIONS confidences].

13. Executive Simplified Summary
We will add a Side-Car brain to intake orders from all channels, standardize rules, and post cleanly into ERP without altering the ERP core. This removes manual rekeying and inconsistent acknowledgements while safely piloting AI on emails, WhatsApp, and fax in Shadow mode. As confidence builds, we will move to Co-Pilot and Autopilot for deterministic steps using evidence-backed thresholds.

## Executive Summary

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Cognitive Friction Analysis

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Architecture of the Future State

_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## Technical Stack

**Clean Core enforcement is explicit: all custom logic is isolated in the Side-Car layer and never embedded in the ERP kernel.**


_Section pending LLM elaboration._

## The Trust Gap Protocol

_Section pending LLM elaboration._


## Appendix: Control and Operability Baseline

Core operability controls include schema validation, deterministic retries, idempotency keys, payload lineage, and cross-system correlation IDs.

## Executive Simplified Summary

This design gives McCain one intelligent intake layer that reduces manual work and speeds order processing across regions. By keeping custom logic in the Side-Car and using only standard ERP APIs, the solution protects SAP stability and lowers upgrade risk. The trust-gated rollout lets the business scale automation safely while improving accuracy, cycle time, and service quality.

pain_points: Manual multi-channel order intake (email/PDF/fax/spreadsheet) is re-keyed into SAP. (Refs: DOC1, DOC2)
Order capture failures require manual triage for EDI/data-quality exceptions. (Refs: DOC1)
Order changes are processed manually in SAP (VA02/VA03) after initial intake. (Refs: DOC1, DOC2)
Order-type branching (standard vs consignment) is resolved manually during entry. (Refs: DOC1, DOC2)
China intake must pass through Digital Hub before SAP posting. (Refs: DOC1)
Uruguay Power Street truck-loading orders require adaptive normalization before ERP posting. (Refs: DOC1)
South Africa indirect orders need next-day Vector backward integration reconciliation. (Refs: DOC1)
Deduction and dispute triage requires manual evidence reconciliation across systems. (Refs: DOC1)

NORTH AMERICA (NA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (exceptions, new SKUs, data sync
failures)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order with order number generated in SAP
Process Note:
• "EDI & Esker are dominant for order intake (Canada & US domestic orders)"
• "OpenText is heavily used for automated entry (Export orders -- JKSEAT region)"
• "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync
failures"
• "For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/ship to and
different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with a full
container load"
• "CSV file transfers are unique to NA, used by Canada domestic for some samples and by
JKSEAT region for customer orders"
Key Comment: "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data
sync failures. For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/
ship to and different incoterms depending on loading WH, when samples are loading along with
a full container load."
Unique Aspect: CSV file processing unique to North America; OpenText boundary system
for export orders; Frequent manual review due to diversity of intake channels and customer
changes
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 usage already established; Need to ensure Esker and
OpenText integration validated in S/4HANA; CSV file handling process must be maintained
 
MEXICO
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service, QSR, Retail), SAP VA01 (Assumption), Edicom/EDI
(Retail), Gmail (Food service)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service via Thalamus) / Automated (EDI for retail)
McCain    5|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System order generated by platform (Thalamus/EDI/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is the main platform for food service and QSR; Edicom EDI is used for retail"
• "Manual entry is common, especially for food service; EDI is automated for retail"
• "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer communication"
• "Order acknowledgment can be automated or manual, and may be requested by the
customer"
• "Common check points: price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate order, requested delivery
date, etc."
Key Comment: "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications and customer
communication"
Unique Aspect: Gmail extensively used for food service operational communications;
Thalamus serves multiple channels (Food service, QSR, Retail); Edicom EDI specific to retail
channel
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus and Edicom EDI integrations must be validated;
Gmail-based workflow formalization needed; VA01 standard transaction confirmed
 
CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean)
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI (Retail), Day & Ross (DRTN
for NA-sourced)
Manual/Automated: Manual (frequent) / Automated (EDI for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record in SAP
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; SAP for system entry"
• "Manual entry is frequent, especially for exports and smaller segments"
• "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks"
• "NA-sourced orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with full traceability for export
compliance"
Key Comment: "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks" and "NA-sourced
orders may integrate with Day & Ross (DRTN)"
Unique Aspect: Export-focused operations with enhanced documentation requirements; Day
& Ross (DRTN) integration for NA-sourced orders; Thalamus for food service segment
SAP Migration Consideration: Export documentation workflows must be preserved; DRTN
integration for NA-sourced orders needs validation; Manual export handling processes require
clear procedures in S/4HANA
 
ANDEAN (Colombia)
System/Tool: Thalamus, Carvajal, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI
Manual/Automated: Manual (portal handling) / Automated (EDI/Carvajal for retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "Thalamus and Carvajal are primary intake platforms; SAP is used for system entry"
McCain    6|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Manual portal handling, especially for food service (Actualmente se esta migrando a
Thalamus)"
• "EDI/Carvajal standards apply for retail and exports"
• "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with data retention for
compliance"
Key Comment: "Manual portal handling, especially for food service.(Actualmente se esta
migrando a Thalamus)"
Unique Aspect: Carvajal platform specific to Andean region for retail; Migration to Thalamus
in progress; EDI/Carvajal standards for retail and exports
SAP Migration Consideration: Thalamus migration ongoing - ensure smooth transition;
Carvajal integration must be validated in S/4HANA; Data retention for compliance mandatory
 
BRAZIL
System/Tool: SAP Fiori (Primary), SAP ECC VA01 (Exceptions: CPU, McD, BK, maritime,
donations/samples), EDI, Neo Grid (Retail)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Fiori entries) / Automated (EDI, Neo Grid)
Performer: CSR (Fiori) / SALES ADM Team (SAP ECC exceptions)
Output: SAP order generated in Fiori or ECC depending on scenario
Process Note:
• "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used for special
cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)"
• "SALES ADM team manages SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
• "WhatsApp/email are common for customer communication and error resolution"
• "Proforma invoices for CPU orders are issued via SAP ECC VF01/VF02"
• "Strong SAP Fiori adoption; exceptions handled manually in ECC"
Key Comment: "SAP Fiori is the main portal for domestic and CPU orders; SAP ECC VA01 is used
for special cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples)" and "SALES ADM team manages
SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders"
Unique Aspect: Dual system architecture (Fiori primary + ECC exceptions); SALES ADM team
handles special scenarios; Strong Fiori adoption among all LATAM regions; WhatsApp business
communication; Proforma invoice generation (VF01/VF02)
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual system (Fiori + ECC) needs consolidation
strategy in S/4HANA; Special handling for CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples must be
accommodated; SALES ADM team workflows require clear definition; Proforma invoice process
(VF01/VF02) validation
 
URUGUAY
System/Tool: Thalamus, Power Street, EDI, SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (domestic via Thalamus/Power Street) / Automated (EDI retail)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: System sales order record
Process Note:
• "EDI, Thalamus, and Power Street are used for order intake; Power Street is unique to
Uruguay"
• "Manual entry is common for domestic orders; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and email are standard for customer communication and error resolution"
McCain    7|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Power Street: Tool to input customer orders for direct and indirect sales. This is used to
load orders on every truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest
of McCain. Orders are loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Key Comment: "Power Street is unique to Uruguay" and "This is used to load orders on every
truck in Uruguay. In this country, sales is a little different from the rest of McCain. Orders are
loaded on the spot (trucks)."
Unique Aspect: Power Street ONLY exists in Uruguay; On-the-spot truck loading model;
Unique sales operational model different from rest of McCain; Direct and indirect sales via
mobile tool
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Power Street unique to Uruguay must be integrated
or replaced in S/4HANA; Mobile truck loading workflow is business-critical; Operational model
significantly different from other regions
 
ARGENTINA
System/Tool: Thalamus (Food service), Krikos360/EDI (Retail), Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone (QSR),
SAP VA01 (Assumption)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Food service, QSR) / Automated (EDI retail via Krikos360)
Performer: CSR / Operations (varies by channel)
Output: System order by platform (Thalamus/Krikos360/SAP)
Process Note:
• "Thalamus is used for food service; Krikos360/EDI for retail; Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone for
QSR"
• "Manual entry is standard for food service and QSR; EDI for retail"
• "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication"
• "Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy; EDI for retail"
Key Comment: "WhatsApp and Gmail are common for rapid customer communication" and
"Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy"
Unique Aspect: Krikos360 platform (Planexware) specific to Argentina retail; Multi-channel
digital communication (Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone) for QSR; Channel-specific tool strategy
SAP Migration Consideration: Krikos360/Planexware integration validation critical; WhatsApp/
Gmail workflow formalization needed; Multi-channel tool landscape requires consolidation
assessment
 
APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand)
System/Tool: Portalink, EDI, Email, SAP VA02/VA03 (NOT VA01 for EDI)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Portalink) / Manual (email-based changes)
Performer: Customer Solutions Team
Output: SAP sales order
Process Note:
• "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders, regardless of
how the original order was received, are processed manually in SAP based on email"
• "Portalink Integration: Serves as critical boundary system converting non-EDI orders into
SAP-compatible format, enabling immediate automated acknowledgements for non-EDI
customers"
• "ANZ: VA01 is not used for EDI order entry. VA02 and VA03 is used"
• "HighRadius Financial Integration: Used for credit checks and payment tracking"
• "Excel Macro Automation: End-of-day order confirmations automated through Excel
macros"
McCain    8|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
• "Z7 CSR Hold Implementation: Universal application of Z7 CSR hold enables manual review
and allocation rule enforcement by commercial team"
Key Comment: "EDI orders are handled automatically in SAP . All changes to customer orders,

and sourcing regions (e.g., NA, EU) run ATP on their side"
Key Comment: "Japan: Customer orders are captured through multiple channels: fax, customer
websites, EDI, and CSV files" and "Orders placed 6 weeks to 4 months in advance" and "No local
ATP/MOQ gating at JKC"
Unique Aspect: Traditional fax still used in Japan; CSV macro/shared folder automation
in KSEAT; Proforma invoice used as order confirmation (not standard acknowledgement);
No local ATP - sourcing regions handle ATP; Long lead times (6 weeks to 4 months); ME21 for
purchase orders alongside VA01; Power BI for shipment tracking; Regional orders 6-16 weeks
ahead of ship date
SAP Migration Consideration: Japan VA01/ME21 usage must be validated; Fax integration/
workflow documentation needed; CSV macro automation from shared folders; Proforma invoice
generation process; No local ATP - sourcing region dependency; Long planning horizon (6
weeks-4 months) must be supported; Power BI integration for tracking
 
GREAT BRITAIN (GB)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption for order creation), EDI, Esker, Email (Manual), ZV05N
(for display)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker) / Manual (email orders)
Performer: Customer Service Representative
Output: Manually entered order (from email) / Automated order from EDI/Esker
Process Note:
• "GB receives orders through three primary channels: EDI (automated), email (manual entry
required), and Esker (PDF document processing)"
• "Transaction codes used: VA01 for order creation, ZV05N for displaying orders, VA02 for
order amendments"
• "EDI orders undergo only basic sense checks; no comprehensive price validation at entry"
• "if there is an error in the EDI it will generate a failed idoc which can be viewed in WE05"
• "Incompletion log in SAP flags missing data, causing EDI orders to fail and go on hold,
equally if the PO is a duplicate SAP puts the order on hold"
• "Manual orders similarly have no price check at entry; incompletion log identifies gaps"
• "Manual process only - no automatic customer notification upon order entry"
• "All orders entered using t-code VA01, where the order type is specified"
Key Comment: "GB receives orders through three primary channels: EDI (automated), email
(manual entry required), and Esker (PDF document processing)" and "Manual process only - no
automatic customer notification upon order entry" and "All orders entered using t-code VA01,
where the order type is specified"
Unique Aspect: ZV05N used for displaying orders (alongside VA03); WE05 for failed IDoc
monitoring; Esker reads PDF documents from customers; No automatic acknowledgement
- manual email confirmation only; No price validation at entry - handled later as claims via
AR inbox; Incompletion log flags missing data; Duplicate PO auto-hold; EDI MDN available on
customer request
SAP Migration Consideration: VA01 standard usage confirmed; ZV05N display transaction
validation in S/4HANA; WE05 IDoc monitoring; Esker PDF integration; Manual acknowledgement
process; No price validation at entry - pricing claims handled downstream; Incompletion log
dependency
 
McCain    19|Signavio GmbH
L4 - Enter Order Into System  
EMENA (Europe, Middle East, North Africa)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), ZVA05N (import transaction), EDI, Esker, CSV process
(Italy), Manual (email/phone)
Manual/Automated: Automated (EDI, Esker, CSV Italy) / Manual (email/phone)
Performer: Customer Service Representative / System (for off-time import)
Output: Orders imported into SAP
Process Note:
• "Order requests could be received via various platforms: Manual entry (email & phone call),
ESKER (reads PDF documents from customers), EDI, CSV process (Italy) via EDI"
• "Orders from various channels are imported into SAP system during off time"
• "Only applicable to EMENA"
• T-codes mentioned: "VA01, VA02, ZVA05N, ZG_MTD_SHIPMENT, VL01N, VL02N, VL06"
Key Comment: "Orders from various channels are imported into SAP system during off time"
and "Only applicable to EMENA"
Unique Aspect: ZVA05N import transaction ONLY in EMENA; Off-time import process
unique to this region; CSV process specific to Italy via EDI; Esker PDF document processing;
Multi-channel intake (EDI, Esker, CSV, Manual); Import batch processing during off-peak hours
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - ZVA05N off-time import transaction unique to
EMENA must be evaluated for S/4HANA equivalent; Italy CSV process via EDI; Esker integration;
Batch import scheduling and timing; Multi-channel consolidation during off-time import
 
SOUTH AFRICA (SA)
System/Tool: SAP VA01 (Assumption), Email (Direct), EDI via Vector (Indirect 3PL)
Manual/Automated: Manual (Direct Network) / Automated (Indirect Network via Vector EDI)
Performer: Customer Service Representative (Direct) / Vector System (Indirect)
Output: Manually entered order (Direct) / EDI order via Vector (Indirect)
Process Note:
• "Purchase requests received via email" (Direct Network)
• "Direct Network: Orders received manually by delivery network"
• "Indirect Network (3PLs/Vector): EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from
customers; next day, orders communicated to McCain for backward integration into SAP
(boundary system), operating on a consignment model"
• "(Direct) Customer Service receives purchase requests via email or manual channels and
manually enters into SAP . T-code VA01 used for order creation"
• "(Indirect) EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from customers"
• "Proforma invoice/manual order confirmation for some customers"
Key Comment: "Direct Network: Orders received manually by delivery network" and "Indirect
Network (3PLs/Vector): EDI captures orders and sends to Vector directly from customers; next
day, orders communicated to McCain for backward integration into SAP (boundary system),
operating on a consignment model"
Unique Aspect: Dual network model - Direct vs Indirect (3PL/Vector); Vector 3PL ONLY in
South Africa; Consignment model for indirect network; Next-day backward integration to
SAP; Proforma invoice for some customers; T-code VA01 confirmed for order creation; Manual
entry for direct network
SAP Migration Consideration: CRITICAL - Dual network model (Direct manual vs Indirect
Vector EDI); Vector 3PL boundary system integration critical; Consignment model logic; Next-day
backward integration timing and process; Proforma invoice generation for specific customers;
VA01 standard usage

SAP ECC (Enterprise Central Component)
SAP ECC (ERP Central Component) is a comprehensive enterprise resource planning (ERP)
software suite developed by SAP . It serves as the core component of many organizations’ IT
landscapes, integrating key business processes and data across departments, and enabling
efficient management of resources, operations, and reporting.Key 
Features of SAP ECC:Centralized database for seamless data flow across business functions
• Modular structure, allowing organizations to implement only the modules they need
• Real-time processing and reporting capabilities
• Strong integration with other SAP and third-party systems
SAP CO, a module within SAP ECC that focuses on internal accounting and management
accounting, providing tools for planning, controlling, and analyzing costs and revenues. It is tightly
integrated with SAP FI (Financial Accounting) and is often referred to as SAP FICO. SAP CO helps
businesses track costs associated with various business processes, allocate costs, and manage
profitability analysis.
