strategy_report_markdown:
# Re-Imagined Strategy Report: Order Intake

1. Executive Summary — One Big Move synthesis
The One Big Move is to introduce a global Side-Car System of Intelligence that normalizes multi-channel order intake, applies deterministic validations and routing, and orchestrates ERP posting while keeping the ERP kernel clean and configuration-led. This hub-and-spoke operating model addresses pervasive manual rekeying, exception handling, and acknowledgement gaps evidenced across regions and channels (email, WhatsApp, fax, portals, EDI) [F-001, F-003, F-004, F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-026, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2]. Deterministic rules will standardize export attributes, order-type branching, Z7 CSR holds, and EDI failure triage within core configuration, while agent personas in the Side-Car handle unstructured intake (email/WhatsApp/fax), change requests, and dispute/deduction evidence reconciliation [F-002, F-019, F-034, F-036; F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-040]. The Trust Gap Protocol starts in Shadow mode to safeguard operations, using confidence levels from Phase 2 path decisions to move toward Co-Pilot and Autopilot only when evidence-backed reliability is achieved [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow; PATH DECISIONS confidences].

2. Cognitive Friction Analysis — The friction table (reproduce as-is)
| Friction_ID | Current_Manual_Action | Where_in_Process | Trigger_or_Input_Channel | Region_Impacted | Systems_or_Tools_Mentioned | Why_It's_Friction | Evidence | Open_Questions |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| F-001 | CSR manually enters/intervenes for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync failures | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | EDI/Esker intake with exceptions; new SKUs; data sync failures | North America (NA) | SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI | Manual exception handling adds delays and error risk; inconsistent throughput | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.5 (NA): "Manual entry and intervention are required for exceptions, new SKUs, or data sync failures" | Frequency/volume of exceptions: Not specified |
| F-002 | CSR performs manual export-related changes (e.g., incoterms when same sold-to/ship-to; samples with full container load) | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Export order scenarios | North America (NA) | SAP VA01 (Assumption), OpenText, EDI | Manual adjustments lead to rework and potential compliance/pricing errors | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.5 (NA): "For export manual changes are required on specific scenarios ex. Same sold to/ship to and different incoterms... when samples are loading along with a full container load" | Specific fields changed and controls applied: Not specified |
| F-003 | CSR conducts frequent manual reviews due to diverse intake channels and customer changes | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Mixed intake (EDI, Esker, CSV) and customer changes | North America (NA) | SAP VA01 (Assumption), Esker, CSV (OpenText), EDI | Increased cognitive load and cycle time; higher chance of oversight | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.5 (NA): "Frequent manual review due to diversity of intake channels and customer changes" | Defined review checklist/criteria: Not specified |
| F-004 | CSR sends manual order acknowledgements and manages notifications via Gmail for food service | Send Order Acknowledgement | Gmail; customer-requested acknowledgements | Mexico | Gmail, Thalamus, Edicom/EDI, SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Email-based steps cause latency and low traceability; inconsistent acknowledgment | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.5 (Mexico): "Heavy reliance on Gmail for food service notifications..." and "Order acknowledgment can be automated or manual, and may be requested by the customer" | Acknowledgement SLA/process: Not specified |
| F-005 | CSR manually enters food service orders | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Food service orders (Thalamus channel) | Mexico | Thalamus, SAP VA01 (Assumption), Edicom/EDI, Gmail | Manual rekeying increases errors and slows processing | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.5 (Mexico): "Manual entry is common, especially for food service" | Whether entry occurs in Thalamus vs SAP: Not specified |
| F-006 | CSR performs manual checks (price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate, requested delivery date) | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Order capture across channels | Mexico | Not specified | Manual validations add time and variability; potential for missed checks | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.5 (Mexico): "Common check points: price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate order, requested delivery date, etc." | Are checks standardized/tooled: Not specified |
| F-007 | CSR prepares extra export documentation and compliance checks manually | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Export orders | CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean) | SAP VA01 (Assumption), Thalamus, Day & Ross (DRTN), EDI | Manual documentation increases cycle time and compliance risk | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6 (CALATIN): "Exports require extra documentation and compliance checks" | Specific documents/tools used: Not specified |
| F-008 | CSR processes corrections and change requests manually with traceability | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Corrections/change requests | CALATIN (Central America & Caribbean) | Not specified | Rework and error potential; manual tracking overhead | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6 (CALATIN): "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with full traceability for export compliance" | Volume and tooling for traceability: Not specified |
| F-009 | CSR manually handles portal orders (food service) and enters into SAP | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Food service portal orders | ANDEAN (Colombia) | Thalamus, Carvajal, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI | Portal handling plus SAP entry adds steps and delay | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6 (ANDEAN): "Manual portal handling, especially for food service.(Actualmente se esta migrando a Thalamus)" | Post-migration residual manual steps: Not specified |
| F-010 | CSR manually handles corrections and change requests with data retention | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Corrections/change requests | ANDEAN (Colombia) | Not specified | Manual rework; effort to maintain compliance data | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6 (ANDEAN): "Corrections and change requests are handled manually, with data retention for compliance" | Volume/approval workflow: Not specified |
| F-011 | SALES ADM team manually enters exceptions/special orders in SAP ECC | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Special cases (CPU, McD, BK, maritime, donations/samples) | Brazil | SAP Fiori, SAP ECC VA01, EDI, Neo Grid | Dual-system handoffs and manual exception path cause delays and errors | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6-7 (Brazil): "SAP ECC VA01 is used for special cases..." and "SALES ADM team manages SAP ECC entries for exceptions and special orders" | Share of orders in exception path: Not specified |
| F-012 | Issue resolution and customer communications handled via WhatsApp/email | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Customer communication/error resolution | Brazil | WhatsApp, Email | Unstructured channels reduce auditability; slower triage | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6-7 (Brazil): "WhatsApp/email are common for customer communication and error resolution" | Integration with case management: Not specified |
| F-013 | Generate and send proforma invoices manually for CPU orders | Not specified | CPU orders | Brazil | SAP ECC VF01/VF02 | Manual document handling adds time; potential for mistakes | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.6-7 (Brazil): "Proforma invoices for CPU orders are issued via SAP ECC VF01/VF02" | Is this part of Enter Order Into System step: Not specified |
| F-014 | CSR performs manual domestic order entry; mobile, on-the-spot order loading on trucks | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Domestic orders; on-the-spot truck loading | Uruguay | Thalamus, Power Street, SAP VA01 (Assumption), EDI | Real-time manual entry increases error risk and audit gaps | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.7 (Uruguay): "Manual entry is common for domestic orders" and "Power Street... used to load orders on every truck... Orders are loaded on the spot (trucks)" | How Power Street syncs to SAP: Not specified |
| F-015 | Manual entry for Food service and QSR orders; multi-channel intake via Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Food service/QSR orders via Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone | Argentina | Thalamus, Krikos360/EDI, Gmail, WhatsApp, Phone, SAP VA01 (Assumption) | Rekeying from unstructured channels leads to delays and errors | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.7 (Argentina): "Manual entry is standard for food service and QSR" and "Gmail/WhatsApp/Phone for QSR" | Consolidation method for multi-channel inputs: Not specified |
| F-016 | CSR conducts extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy orders | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Retail/hardcopy | Argentina | Krikos360/EDI | Manual reviews increase cycle time and cost | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.7 (Argentina): "Extensive manual review for retail/hardcopy" | Definition of "hardcopy" and review criteria: Not specified |
| F-017 | Manually process all order changes in SAP based on email instructions | Enter Standard Order Details into the ERP | Email-based change requests | APAC-ANZ (Australia & New Zealand) | SAP VA02/VA03, Email, EDI, Portalink | Email-driven updates cause rework, delays, and audit gaps | L4 - Enter Order Into System, p.8 (ANZ): "All changes to customer orders... are processed manually in SAP based on email" | Standard templates/controls for change emails: Not specified |

4. Architecture of the Future State — Hub-and-spoke operating model with agent personas
Architecture principle: Clean Core is enforced end-to-end; all custom logic, perception models, and orchestration reside in a Side-Car System of Intelligence. The ERP kernel (SAP ECC/VA01/VA02/VA03) remains configuration-led for validations, order-type branching, Z7 CSR holds, output determination, and IDoc-based posting [F-019, F-035, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].

Hub-and-spoke model:
- Hub: Side-Car Order Intake Orchestrator
  - Functions: multi-channel intake normalization (email, WhatsApp, fax, CSV, portal payloads), deterministic validations, path routing (A/B/C), exception queuing, acknowledgement dispatch, and ERP posting via integration contracts [F-004, F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-024, F-026, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Clean Core enforcement: no custom code in ERP kernel; all AI, parsing, and workflow logic live in the Side-Car [Clean Core principle; supports ERP rules for order type and Z7 holds per DOC1 references F-019, F-036].
- Spokes (intake channels and boundary systems):
  - EDI/IDoc spoke: deterministic intake for standard orders; IDoc failure triage via Side-Car using error-code rules [F-001, F-034; DOC1].
  - Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360 spoke: structured channel integration; Side-Car applies validations and routes exceptions [F-001, F-003, F-016, F-026, F-028; DOC1].
  - Digital Hub (China) spoke: mandatory gateway; Side-Car integrates to reduce manual portal entry and automate validations ahead of SAP posting [F-020, F-037; DOC1].
  - Thalamus/Carvajal/Neo Grid spoke: food service portals; Side-Car maps portal orders deterministically into ERP [F-005, F-009, F-011; DOC1].
  - Power Street mobile spoke: truck-loading mobile orders; Side-Car normalizes payload variability before posting [F-014, F-038; DOC1].
  - Vector (SA indirect) spoke: reconciliation with next-day backward integration; Side-Car coordinates and surfaces exceptions [F-039; DOC1].
  - Unstructured channels: email, WhatsApp, fax, phone, customer websites; Side-Car uses agent personas to parse and normalize [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-026, F-028, F-029, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Agent personas in the Side-Car:
  - Intake Scribe: OCR/NLP-enabled extraction for email/WhatsApp/fax/phone transcripts; transforms unstructured artifacts into normalized order payloads with confidence tagging [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Intent Analyzer: deterministic classification and routing for order type (standard vs consignment), Z7 CSR holds, export incoterms fields, and duplicate detection [F-002, F-018, F-019, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Dispute Judge: triage for deductions/claims requiring evidence reconciliation across systems; assembles case context and recommendations for CSR approval [F-040; DOC1].

ERP responsibilities (configuration-led):
- Order-type branching and validations remain in ERP config, not custom code [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Z7 CSR hold enforcement and allocation rules via standard gating, with Side-Car triggering and monitoring [F-019; DOC1].
- Output determination for proforma invoicing and acknowledgements configured in ERP or standard middleware outputs; Side-Car orchestrates dispatch [F-013, F-025, F-027; DOC1].

Traceability to evidence:
- Hub-and-spoke integrates every listed regional channel, aligning with documented systems and manual steps [F-001–F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
- Agent personas are scoped to replace manual steps where unstructured content and reasoning are documented frictions [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-040; DOC1; DOC2].

5. Technical Stack — System of Intelligence, System of Record, integration contracts
System of Record:
- ERP: SAP ECC with transactions VA01/VA02/VA03 and billing VF01/VF02, serving as the authoritative order record and posting target [F-011, F-013, F-017, F-026, F-035; DOC1].
- EDI/IDoc processing: ERP-side IDoc channels for automated ingestion and error handling with deterministic rules [F-034; DOC1].

System of Intelligence (Side-Car):
- Intake adapters: connectors for EDI/Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360, Digital Hub (China), Thalamus, Carvajal, Neo Grid, Power Street, Vector, and unstructured sources (email, WhatsApp, fax, phone, customer websites) [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-009–F-012, F-014–F-016, F-020–F-026, F-028–F-029, F-037–F-039; DOC1; DOC2].
- Parsing and normalization: AI-enabled services embedded in Side-Car for unstructured channels; deterministic mappers for structured channel payloads [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Validation engine: business-rule services for price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate checks, requested delivery date, and export attributes; implemented outside ERP to preserve Clean Core [F-002, F-006, F-018, F-019, F-036; DOC1].
- Acknowledgement and notification orchestration: standard dispatchers handling automated acknowledgements, proforma confirmations, and customer notifications [F-004, F-022, F-025, F-027; DOC1].
- Case and dispute triage: Dispute Judge service for cross-system evidence reconciliation, creating recommended actions for CSR approval [F-040; DOC1].

Integration contracts:
- Staged processing contract between Side-Car and ERP:
  - Intake stage: unify payload schemas across channels (EDI, portal, mobile, unstructured) [F-001, F-003, F-014, F-020, F-033–F-038; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Classification stage: determine order type, holds, export attributes, and duplicates using deterministic rules [F-002, F-018–F-019, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Posting stage: create or amend orders via ERP API/IDoc/transactions, maintaining idempotency and Clean Core [F-017, F-035; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Acknowledgement stage: issue confirmations via configured outputs, avoiding manual email steps [F-004, F-022, F-025, F-027; DOC1].
- China-specific gateway contract: enforce Digital Hub as a mandatory upstream stage and align validations to reduce manual entry in Digital Hub before SAP posting [F-020, F-037; DOC1].
- Uruguay mobile contract: normalize Power Street payloads and support same-day posting workflows [F-014, F-038; DOC1].
- South Africa reconciliation contract: schedule Vector backward integration reconciliation and surface exceptions next day [F-039; DOC1].

Clean Core enforcement:
- All custom logic (AI parsing, cross-channel normalization, workflow orchestration) is strictly in the Side-Car; ERP uses only configuration and standard interfaces [Clean Core principle; aligns with evidence of ERP transactions and IDoc workflows in DOC1/DOC2].

6. Integration Design Deep Dive — Staged processing contract (intake, classification, ERP posting)
Stage 0: Intake and normalization (Side-Car)
- Channel adapters receive orders and change requests from EDI/Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360, Digital Hub (China), Thalamus/Carvajal/Neo Grid, Power Street, Vector, and unstructured sources (email, WhatsApp, fax, phone, websites) [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-009–F-012, F-014–F-016, F-020–F-026, F-028–F-029, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Unstructured parsing via Intake Scribe converts email/WhatsApp/fax/phone/web artifacts into normalized payloads with confidence scores and extracted fields (sold-to, ship-to, SKU, quantity, incoterms, RDD) [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Digital Hub (China) intake respects mandatory gateway, reducing manual typing by integrating upload/mapping from Side-Car where allowable by the portal’s interface [F-020, F-037; DOC1].

Stage 1: Deterministic classification (Side-Car)
- Order-type determination (standard vs consignment) based on business rules, removing inconsistent manual branching [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Z7 CSR hold rules applied deterministically before ERP posting, standardizing allocation enforcement [F-019; DOC1].
- Export-specific attributes (incoterms when same sold-to/ship-to; samples with full container load) standardized via core rules [F-002; DOC1].
- Duplicate detection for EDI orders preventing manual deletion and accidental removal [F-018; DOC1].

Stage 2: Validation (Side-Car and ERP configuration)
- Price, route, warehouse, weight, duplicate checks, requested delivery date validations applied deterministically; ERP retains core validations; Side-Car performs pre-validation to reduce SAP error cycles [F-006; DOC1].
- EDI/data-quality exception triage: use error-code-based rules to route and resolve failures; improve first pass automation [F-034; DOC1].

Stage 3: ERP posting (Clean Core)
- Sales order creation/amendment via standard interfaces (IDoc/API) or transactions VA01/VA02/VA03 as per region-specific process, avoiding any custom ERP kernel code [F-017, F-026, F-035; DOC1; DOC2].
- China: Side-Car triggers EDI-based creation after Digital Hub validations, preserving mandatory gateway compliance [F-020, F-037; DOC1].
- Uruguay: Side-Car posts normalized Power Street orders same-day with idempotent keys to prevent duplicates [F-014, F-038; DOC1].

Stage 4: Acknowledgements and outputs
- Automated order acknowledgements replace Gmail/manual steps across regions [F-004, F-022, F-027; DOC1].
- Proforma invoice generation for CPU and KC confirmations via configured outputs and automated dispatch, removing manual PDF handling [F-013, F-025; DOC1].

Stage 5: Exceptions, changes, and disputes
- Changes based on email instructions parsed by Intake Scribe and routed to deterministic VA02/VA03 updates; CSR approves if confidence is below threshold [F-017, F-035; DOC1; DOC2].
- Dispute/Deduction triage assembles cross-system evidence and proposes actions for CSR adjudication [F-040; DOC1].

Idempotency and deduplication baked into every stage to address duplicate EDI orders and repeated manual touchpoints documented in the process graphs [F-018, F-030–F-031; DOC1; DOC2].

7. Agent Persona Reasoning Model — Intake Scribe, Intent Analyzer, Dispute Judge boundaries
Intake Scribe (Perception + Extraction):
- Scope: unstructured order intake and change requests from email, WhatsApp, fax, phone transcripts, and customer websites [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-023, F-028, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
- Output: normalized order/change payloads with field-level confidence and provenance.
- Boundary: does not post to ERP; hands off to Intent Analyzer and ERP workflow per Trust Gap phase (Shadow) [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow].

Intent Analyzer (Deterministic Classification + Routing):
- Scope: order-type classification (standard vs consignment), Z7 CSR hold enforcement, export attributes normalization, duplicate detection [F-002, F-018–F-019, F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Output: classification decisions and routing to ERP posting or exception queues.
- Boundary: relies on rules; no custom logic in ERP kernel; executes fully in Side-Car.

Dispute Judge (Evidence Triage + Recommendation):
- Scope: deduction and dispute triage requiring cross-system reconciliation and compliance context assembly [F-040; DOC1].
- Output: recommended resolution actions, evidence bundles, and CSR decision support.
- Boundary: never auto-posts financial adjustments; operates under Shadow/Co-Pilot until confidence thresholds are met.

Agent-to-path alignment:
- Path A: ERP configuration-led determinations (e.g., order-type, Z7 hold); Intent Analyzer orchestrates using deterministic rules [F-019, F-036; PATH A rationales].
- Path B: deterministic integrations/validations; Intake Scribe may help normalize structured payloads; Intent Analyzer enforces rules [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-007–F-011, F-014, F-016–F-018, F-020–F-029, F-030–F-035, F-037, F-039; PATH B rationales].
- Path C: perception and adaptive handling required; Intake Scribe leads with NLP/OCR; Dispute Judge engages for cross-system evidence [F-012, F-015, F-033, F-038, F-040; PATH C rationales].

8. The Trust Gap Protocol — Shadow / Co-Pilot / Autopilot phases with confidence thresholds
Phases:
- Shadow: agents observe, parse, and recommend; no autonomous ERP postings; CSRs retain full control [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow]. Apply recommendations for unstructured intake and changes with confidence tags derived from Path Decisions (e.g., 90%–97% confidences).
- Co-Pilot: agents propose actions and pre-stage transactions; CSR approves before posting. Trigger Co-Pilot for cases where confidence regularly meets or exceeds documented levels (e.g., 90%+ for deterministic validations; 97%+ for structured EDI triage per Path Decisions) [PATH DECISIONS confidences].
- Autopilot: agents act without human approval on deterministic flows where evidence and historical performance sustain high confidence (e.g., >97% confidence for duplicates and EDI exception rule triage per Path Decisions) [F-018, F-034; PATH DECISIONS confidences].

Confidence thresholds (evidence-linked):
- Deterministic validations and routing: adopt Co-Pilot at ≥90% confidence; consider Autopilot only when ≥97% confidence sustained and exceptions trend to zero [PATH DECISIONS entries citing 90% and 97%].
- Unstructured intake parsing (email/WhatsApp/fax): remain in Shadow until ≥97% extraction confidence consistently achieved and CSR override rates materially decline [F-012, F-015, F-023, F-033; PATH C confidences].
- Dispute triage: Shadow-to-Co-Pilot only; Autopilot gated due to compliance sensitivity, regardless of confidence [F-040].

Clean Core guardrails:
- Even in Autopilot, ERP kernel remains configuration-only; Side-Car executes orchestration and posts via standard interfaces [DOC1; DOC2].

- F-030 — Path B: Repeated manual touchpoints can be converted to deterministic workflows; no inherent need for perception or reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)

- F-032 — Path A: Decision gateways should be standardized in core ERP process logic; rule-based branching removes variance. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-033 — Path C: Manual intake from email/PDF/fax requires perception and adaptive mapping; multi-step reasoning for exceptions. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-034 — Path B: EDI/IDoc failure triage can be deterministic (error codes, validation rules); no unstructured perception needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-035 — Path B: Order amendments via SAP transactions follow rule-based steps; workflow/RPA suitable; no perception/reasoning needed. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-036 — Path A: Order type classification is a core ERP decision; standardize branching rules in ERP (deterministic). Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-037 — Path B: Mandatory Digital Hub gateway implies deterministic validations/routing; automate within/around the portal. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 97%)
- F-038 — Path C: Mobile payload variability from Power Street indicates adaptive action is needed to normalize and handle exceptions. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-039 — Path B: Next-day 3PL backward integration reconciliation is deterministic; workflow automation suffices. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)
- F-040 — Path C: Dispute/deduction triage involves cross-system evidence and likely unstructured artifacts; requires perception and reasoning. Classified via mandatory Phase 2 suitability assessment. (confidence 90%)

10. Regional Policy Registry — JSON block of regional nuances
{
  "india_dc_based_entry": true,
  "china_digital_hub": true,
  "anz_va01_exception_enabled": true,
  "uruguay_power_street_enabled": true
}

11. Delivery and Rollout Plan — Wave 1/2/3 with KPIs, exit criteria, and governance
Delivery approach adheres to Clean Core: Side-Car is the change vehicle; ERP remains configuration-led and untouched by custom code [DOC1; DOC2].

- Wave 1: Stabilize deterministic core and standardize acknowledgements
  - Scope: Path A frictions and foundational Path B with clear deterministic rules (e.g., export fields, Z7 CSR hold, order-type branching, duplicate detection, EDI exception rule triage, automated acknowledgements, proforma outputs) [F-002, F-018–F-019, F-032, F-034; F-004, F-022, F-025, F-027; DOC1; DOC2].
  - KPIs:
    - First-pass automation rate for EDI and deterministic validations (derived from reduction in manual triage and deletion) [F-018, F-034].
    - Automated acknowledgement coverage across regions (replacement of manual Gmail/email steps) [F-004, F-022, F-027].
    - Elimination of inconsistent order-type branching by ERP configuration [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Exit criteria:
    - All Path A items operationalized with documented rules and measurable reduction of manual steps.
    - Side-Car contracts for acknowledgements and proforma outputs functioning across applicable regions [F-013, F-025].
  - Governance:
    - Business Process Owner sign-off per friction ID.
    - ERP Configuration Board approval for any rule changes, ensuring kernel cleanliness.

- Wave 2: Integrate structured portals and regional gateways
  - Scope: Path B frictions for portals and gateways (Esker/OpenText/Portalink/Krikos360, Thalamus/Carvajal/Neo Grid, Digital Hub, Vector, Power Street normalization) [F-001, F-003–F-005, F-007–F-011, F-014, F-016, F-020, F-021, F-029, F-037, F-039; DOC1].
  - KPIs:
    - Reduction in manual rekeying for food service/QSR and portal orders [F-005, F-009, F-015].
    - Digital Hub manual entry elimination rate (China) [F-020, F-037].
    - Same-day posting rate for Power Street mobile orders (idempotent, normalized) [F-014, F-038].
  - Exit criteria:
    - Side-Car adapters live for each portal/gateway with validated mappings.
    - Documented exception queues and resolutions for remaining manual steps.
  - Governance:
    - Regional SteerCos approve go-lives aligned with nuances JSON (e.g., china_digital_hub true; uruguay_power_street_enabled true).

- Wave 3: Unstructured channels and dispute triage
  - Scope: Path C frictions (WhatsApp/email intake, multi-channel PDF/fax/spreadsheet, Power Street adaptive normalization, deductions/disputes) [F-012, F-015, F-033, F-038, F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
  - KPIs:
    - Extraction confidence for email/WhatsApp/fax orders (agent-level confidence aligned with Path Decisions) [PATH C confidences].
    - CSR override rate for parsed changes and unstructured orders (trend toward Co-Pilot viability) [F-017; DOC2].
    - Dispute cycle-time reduction via evidence bundling [F-040; DOC1].
  - Exit criteria:
    - Shadow mode stable with documented confidence metrics; progression criteria to Co-Pilot defined by Trust Gap thresholds.
    - Proven triage recommendations by Dispute Judge with CSR acceptance rate targets.
  - Governance:
    - Trust Gap Council evaluates evidence to approve phase transitions; Clean Core gate enforces Side-Car-only logic.

Global governance and controls:
- A Friction ID catalog drives scope and acceptance; each ID maps to path, region, and evidence tag [F-001–F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
- Operability SRE team monitors idempotency, retries, and schema validation across stages and channels, consistent with Appendix controls.

12. Appendix: Control and Operability Baseline — Schema validation, retries, idempotency, governance
Controls (applied uniformly in Side-Car to preserve Clean Core):
- Schema validation:
  - Unified payload schema versioning for EDI, portal, mobile, and parsed unstructured content to reduce rework from format errors evidenced across diverse intake channels [F-003, F-033; DOC1; DOC2].
  - Region-specific extensions for Digital Hub, Power Street, and Vector, documented against evidence of mandatory gateways and mobile variability [F-020, F-037, F-038, F-039; DOC1].
- Idempotency:
  - Idempotent keys (e.g., source reference + customer + timestamp) to prevent duplicate ERP postings and align with duplicate EDI deletion friction [F-018; DOC1].
  - Idempotent change processing with compare-and-apply semantics for email-based changes [F-017; DOC2].
- Retries and backoff:
  - Deterministic retry policies for transient EDI/IDoc errors and portal integrations, guided by error-code triage evidence [F-034; DOC1].
  - Circuit breakers for Digital Hub and Vector integrations to avoid cascading failures [F-037, F-039; DOC1].
- Exception queues:
  - Dedicated queues for unstructured parsing low-confidence artifacts (email/WhatsApp/fax/phone), disputable changes, and compliance-sensitive cases [F-012, F-015, F-017, F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
  - SLA tracking for manual acknowledgements transitioning to automated, aligned to evidence of current manual processes [F-004, F-022, F-027; DOC1].
- Governance and audit:
  - Full provenance on all transformed fields (with source artifact links) to address auditability gaps from unstructured channels [F-012, F-015; DOC1].
  - ERP remains configuration-driven; any change in validations or branching is approved by ERP Configuration Board, respecting Clean Core [F-036; DOC1; DOC2].
- Security and compliance:
  - Data minimization and encryption for WhatsApp/email artifacts; evidence-driven need to secure unstructured communications [F-012; DOC1].
  - Export documentation workflows centralized, reducing manual preparation and compliance risk [F-007; DOC1].
- Operability dashboards:
  - Channel health, exception rates, first-pass automation, acknowledgement coverage, and dispute triage metrics mapped to friction IDs [F-001–F-040; DOC1; DOC2].
- Testing and release management:
  - Evidence-linked test cases per friction ID; dual-run Shadow mode validation before enabling Co-Pilot/Autopilot per Trust Gap [Trust Gap Phase: Shadow; PATH DECISIONS confidences].

13. Executive Simplified Summary
We will add a Side-Car brain to intake orders from all channels, standardize rules, and post cleanly into ERP without altering the ERP core. This removes manual rekeying and inconsistent acknowledgements while safely piloting AI on emails, WhatsApp, and fax in Shadow mode. As confidence builds, we will move to Co-Pilot and Autopilot for deterministic steps using evidence-backed thresholds.

**Clean Core enforcement is explicit: all custom logic is isolated in the Side-Car layer and never embedded in the ERP kernel.**

## Appendix: Control and Operability Baseline

Core operability controls include schema validation, deterministic retries, idempotency keys, payload lineage, and cross-system correlation IDs.

## Executive Simplified Summary

This design gives McCain one intelligent intake layer that reduces manual work and speeds order processing across regions. By keeping custom logic in the Side-Car and using only standard ERP APIs, the solution protects SAP stability and lowers upgrade risk. The trust-gated rollout lets the business scale automation safely while improving accuracy, cycle time, and service quality.

path_classifier_prompt:
TASK: Path Classification (A/B/C) for Friction Points (Table Output Only)

Role: Principal Enterprise AI Architect and Business Transformation Strategist.
Mode: High reasoning.

Goal
Classify each friction point into exactly one of three paths:
- Path A (Core Standardization): revert/force to standard ERP/CRM functionality.
- Path B (Platform Automation): deterministic automation (workflows/RPA).
- Path C (Agentic AI Deployment): probabilistic reasoning (GenAI agents).

Inputs
You will receive:
1) A "Cognitive Friction Analysis" table of friction points
2) The same source documents (for evidence cross-check)

Suitability Rule (STRICT — must be explicit in justification)
Assign Path C ONLY if the task requires at least one:
- Perception (reading/extracting unstructured inputs like emails/PDF/free text)
- Reasoning (trade-off analysis, multi-step judgment)
- Adaptive Action (dynamic exception handling/planning)
If not, prefer Path B or Path A.

Output (STRICT)
Produce ONLY a JSON array titled "path_classifications".
Each element must have these keys:
- friction_id
- recommended_path (A / B / C)
- suitability_justification (1–2 lines; MUST reference rule-based vs perception/reasoning/adaptive action)
- core_vs_sidecar_orientation (Core / Side-Car / Hybrid)
- human_supervision_needed (Yes / No / Conditional)
- confidence (High / Medium / Low)
- evidence (Document + page/section supporting classification)
- open_questions (if classification depends on missing info, else empty string)

Rules
- Map 1:1 to the Friction_IDs from the friction table.
- Use evidence from the provided documents.
- Do not invent technologies or tools not stated in the documents.
- Keep "Core vs Side-Car" conceptual (no vendor/tool names unless stated).

Begin
Classify all friction points and output the JSON array only.
